confirmations.reason: The feature described is 'Compaction', which manages context window limits by summarizing/compacting long conversation history — this is context compaction, not context caching. Prompt/context caching refers to storing and reusing repeated prompt prefixes to reduce latency and cost. These are distinct mechanisms; compaction does not realize the caching capability.

The 'Approval Policy' feature controls when the agent must pause and seek user approval before crossing sandbox boundaries (e.g., network access, file writes). This is an approval/safety gate, not a plan-then-execute mode. Plan-then-execute requires the agent to produce a reviewable plan before making any edits — a fundamentally different workflow than interrupt-on-policy-violation.

'Organization and project-level access scoping' governs who can access what resources — it is an authorization/permissions feature. It does not provide token spend tracking, usage dashboards, or cost caps, which are what the cost & usage tracking capability requires.

'Saved state' is too vague to confirm a direct realization without more detail, but as described it does not explicitly provide save points with the ability to roll back or undo agent changes. Checkpoint/rewind requires explicit snapshotting of agent state and the ability to restore a prior state — a generic 'saved state' label does not confirm this specific rollback/undo mechanic is present.
